title: A Day as a Manufacturing Automation Engineer
setting: Major food processing plant, Western Sydney NSW, with robotic palletising and packaging lines
companySize: Mid-size manufacturer (800 employees, 12-person automation engineering team)
description: Marcus Rivera is a Senior Automation Engineer responsible for the robotic systems at a food processing facility that produces snack foods for Australian supermarkets. He manages a fleet of 8 industrial robots (ABB and FANUC) and 4 collaborative robots (Universal Robots UR10e) across three production lines. His work includes programming new robot tasks, troubleshooting breakdowns, optimising cycle times, integrating new equipment, and ensuring all robotic systems meet Australian safety standards. He is currently leading a project to install a new vision-guided cobot cell for quality inspection.
salary: $110,000 - $145,000 AUD per year (plus overtime and on-call allowances)

schedule.activity: Arrive at plant, check overnight production reports
schedule.note: Night shift reported a gripper fault on the palletising robot in Line 2 — parts are being hand-stacked, reducing throughput by 40%

schedule.activity: Diagnose and fix the palletising robot fault
schedule.note: Vacuum cup worn out on the gripper — replace cup, recalibrate vacuum sensor threshold, test with product

schedule.activity: Morning meeting with production manager and maintenance team
schedule.note: Review production targets, planned maintenance windows, and the timeline for the new inspection cell installation

schedule.activity: Program a new product pattern on the palletiser
schedule.note: New product size launching next week — design and simulate a new pallet stacking pattern that maximises boxes per pallet within weight limits

schedule.activity: Commission the new cobot inspection cell
schedule.note: Integrating a UR10e with a Cognex camera for visual quality inspection — checking seal integrity on snack food packaging

schedule.activity: Risk assessment for the cobot cell with the safety officer
schedule.note: Conducting a formal risk assessment per ISO/TS 15066 — measuring reach distances, calculating force limits, defining safety zones
Eat in the break room with production operators — good opportunity to hear about small issues before they become big problems

schedule.activity: PLC programming for the new cell integration
schedule.note: Programming the PLC logic that coordinates the cobot cell with the upstream conveyor and downstream packaging machine
Line 1 is 2 seconds below target cycle time — analysing robot paths to find time savings without increasing speed beyond safe limits
Teaching two operators how to perform basic cobot recovery (clearing faults, restarting programmes) so they do not need to call engineering for every stoppage
Updating the robot maintenance schedule, documenting today's fixes, and ordering spare parts for preventive maintenance next week

schedule.activity: Handover notes for night shift, head home
schedule.note: On-call tonight — phone must be on in case of critical robot faults that night shift cannot resolve

responsibilities: Program, commission, and maintain industrial and collaborative robots across multiple production lines
Troubleshoot robot faults and minimise production downtime
Optimise robot cycle times and production throughput
Ensure all robotic systems comply with Australian safety standards (AS/NZS 4024, ISO 10218, ISO/TS 15066)
Lead automation projects from concept through to commissioning
Integrate robots with PLCs, conveyors, vision systems, and other production equipment
Train production operators on basic robot operation and fault recovery
Manage spare parts inventory and preventive maintenance schedules
challenges: Minimising production downtime — every minute a robot is stopped costs real money in lost output
Working within tight capital budgets — the business wants automation ROI in under 2 years
Coordinating robot maintenance windows with production schedules — machines run almost 24/7
Keeping skills current across multiple robot brands (ABB, FANUC, UR) each with different programming environments
Managing the on-call responsibility — robot breakdowns do not respect work-life boundaries
Navigating safety compliance while meeting aggressive production targets
rewards: Direct, tangible impact — you see the results of your work running on the factory floor every day
Variety — no two days are the same; a mix of hands-on work, programming, and project management
Problem solving under pressure — diagnosing and fixing a robot fault while production is waiting is an adrenaline rush
Job security — demand for manufacturing automation engineers in Australia consistently exceeds supply
Career progression into engineering management, plant management, or specialist consulting
The satisfaction of seeing a new automated cell you designed and built running reliably in production
careerPath: Junior Automation Engineer → Senior Automation Engineer → Automation Engineering Manager → Plant Engineering Manager
Alternative: specialise as a robot systems integrator, designing and building complete automated cells for multiple clients
Alternative: move into a robot manufacturer's application engineering team (ABB, FANUC, Universal Robots)
Alternative: start an automation consulting business serving Australian SMB manufacturers
